title: A Carousel with a Story: Smithsonian's Ride Reopens After 3 Years
summary:
After being closed for almost three years, the Smithsonian carousel on the National Mall in Washington, D.C., is spinning again! But this isn't just any merry-go-round. It has a really cool and important story.

Back in the 1960s, this carousel was at an amusement park in Maryland called Gwynn Oak. Back then, the park only let white people in. That was unfair, so brave kids and adults protested. Finally, on August 28, 1963 — the same day Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. gave his famous "I Have a Dream" speech — the park opened to everyone.

Sharon Langley was the first Black child to ride the carousel that day. She was only 11 months old! Now, 63 years later, she rode again on a horse called "Freedom Rider." She said the carousel belongs near all the monuments of freedom.

The carousel has 54 horses, a sea monster, a pig, and two chariots. Kids love it. Seven-year-old Lucas Platt said, "It's actually one of the fastest carousels I've really been on. It's great!"

So next time you see a carousel, remember: it's not just about going round and round. It's about fun and fairness for everyone.
why_it_matters: This story reminds kids that everyday things like a carousel can have a powerful history about fairness — and that even young people can be part of making the world a better place.

keywords.explanation: The museum group that owns the carousel on the National Mall in Washington, D.C.

The first Black child to ride the carousel after the park was integrated in 1963.

options: It only let white people in.

background_read: During the 1950s and 1960s, many places in the United States were segregated — Black people and white people had to use different schools, restaurants, and parks.
Civil rights activists, including children, held protests to demand fairness. In 1963, their efforts led to laws that made segregation illegal.
The carousel's story shows that even simple things like a merry-go-round can be part of the fight for equality.
Article_Structure: WHO: The Smithsonian Institution reopened the carousel; Sharon Langley, who was the first Black child to ride it in 1963, rode it again.
WHAT: The Smithsonian carousel on the National Mall reopened after three years of restoration.
WHERE: The National Mall in Washington, D.C., in front of the Arts and Industries Building.
WHY: The carousel is a symbol of civil rights — it was originally at a segregated park that opened to everyone on the same day Dr. King gave his famous speech.

perspectives.description: Kids and families are excited to ride the carousel again. It's a fun, fast ride that everyone can enjoy.
Many people see the carousel as a living monument to the civil rights movement. Sharon Langley's return shows how far we've come.
The story teaches us that segregation was unfair and that brave people fought to change it. The carousel is a symbol of that struggle.
